notes.anchor: I have set out everything in full in the official dispatch
notes.note: Roman provincial governors sent formal dispatches (litterae publicae or tabulae) to the Senate distinct from private correspondence; Plancus distinguishes this personal note to Cicero from his official report.

notes.anchor: M. Varisidius, a Roman knight and a close friend of mine
notes.note: A Roman knight (eques Romanus) was a member of the second property-census class, below senators; equites frequently served as officers, agents, and envoys, and Plancus uses Varisidius here as a trusted verbal messenger to supplement the written letter.

notes.anchor: while others looked to be occupying the ground of glory
notes.note: Plancus obliquely refers to governors such as Decimus Brutus (besieged at Mutina) who had already declared openly for the Senate; he explains his own strategic delay as a considered choice to maximize impact rather than timidity.

notes.anchor: the rewards whose prospect you used to spur me to glory
notes.note: Plancus acknowledges Cicero's sustained lobbying campaign in which the orator had urged provincial commanders to declare for the Senate, dangling the prospect of public honours, triumphs, and posthumous gloria.
